macro_summary: Tuesday's session saw the S&P 500 recover 0.49% after a surprisingly cool June CPI print (3.7% YoY, down from 4.3%) eased Fed rate-hike fears, though the rally was tempered by a renewed Hormuz blockade and a proposed 20% transit fee that pushed WTI above $80. Semiconductor and memory names surged on fresh analyst upgrades and a revival of AI capex optimism, while ASML climbed 1.8% after-hours on its earnings release. The macro backdrop remains split between disinflationary relief and escalating energy-supply risk, keeping the VIX below 17 for now.
commodities_note: WTI settled at $80.19, up 14% over five days as the US reinstated its Iran naval blockade and proposed a Hormuz toll—traffic through the strait is near all-time lows. Gold held firm at $4,041, while the 10-year yield eased to 4.59% after the CPI miss. Oil's surge is the dominant cross-asset signal; sustained $80+ crude raises input-cost risks for semis and AI infrastructure names.

writeup.headline: GaN oversold at RSI 34, but the 12-day earnings binary and lack of a clear catalyst cap the signal.
writeup.what_to_do: NVTS inched 2.3% higher to $13.09, now 37% below its 50-day. The pre-earnings band (12 days out) shows a wide 13.2% avg up / -18.6% avg down range, but without a thesis break resolved, the stock is just a volatile bounce candidate. Reassess after the Jul 27 print if commercial GaN design wins are announced. Relative strength: lagging AI Power Infrastructure by 24.4% over the month.
writeup.thesis_break_condition: Thesis breaks if NVTS Jul 27 earnings miss by >5% or guidance is cut.

writeup.headline: 64x forward PE and a tight ceiling — PLTR's Defense moat doesn't justify the multiple.
writeup.what_to_do: PLTR added 3.8% to $133.72, turning positive above its 50-day. The DoD Maven contract and Army Enterprise Agreement are long-term tailwinds, but the stock faces stiff resistance at $136.10, and the 3.6:1 distorted headline R:R masks a 0.5:1 sizing R:R using $128.75 support. The Aug 3 Q2 print is the next validation of the $7.65B guide — wait for a pullback toward $130 before considering entry.
writeup.entry_block: CAUTION: forward P/E of 64x (>40x). Requires explicit justification of why earnings growth supports this multiple — default to WATCH unless thesis is clearly articulated with specific catalysts and timeline.
writeup.valuation_judgment: Trades at a 157% premium to the BigTech cluster median — only a sustained 30%+ DoD revenue CAGR can absorb that premium.
writeup.thesis_break_condition: Thesis breaks if multiple compresses toward the BigTech cluster median (24.8x) without a corresponding earnings re-rating.

thesis_highlights: WeRide-Lenovo 200,000-vehicle Level 4 deal (Auto China 2026) — commercial traction building; verify revenue-per-vehicle trajectory in earnings.
ADR/China delisting risk is a standing consideration — always maintain starter sizing for WRD.

writeup.headline: 5d +12.7% momentum block — short-squeeze, not fundamental re-rate, on the Lenovo partner deal.
writeup.what_to_do: WRD popped 7% to $6.12, continuing a volatile bounce. The Lenovo 200k-vehicle L4 deal is real commercial traction, but the ADR/China delisting risk persists and revenue-per-vehicle monetization remains unproven. No entry after a +13% 5-day move; wait for consolidation toward $6.03 support or the Jul 30 Q2 print for fundamental clarity.

writeup.headline: Upgraded to WATCH — near support at $609, R:R 9:1 distorted, but sizing R:R 2.2:1 passes.
writeup.what_to_do: SITM rallied 4.5% to $624, but remains 10.6% below its 50-day. The MEMS timing displacement thesis is a multi-year growth story, and the wide-stop R:R using $564 structural support is a clean 2.2:1 to $758 resistance. Entry zone $610–$625, invalidation below $609; the path to $758 is intact once the stock reclaims its 50-day with volume. Starter position only — the forward PE of 51x and pending Aug 5 earnings demand sizing discipline.
writeup.entry_block: CAUTION: forward P/E of 51x (>40x). Requires explicit justification of why earnings growth supports this multiple — default to WATCH unless thesis is clearly articulated with specific catalysts and timeline.

singapore.summary: All three banks remain hard-blocked on extension with RSIs above 84 — a unified cluster-CAUTION driven by the Q2 GDP beat and a broader re-rate of Singapore equities rather than NIM improvement. The divergence within the cluster is OCBC's outperformance (best profit growth) vs UOB's revenue contraction, but the structural NIM compression thesis persists as both US rates and MAS policy keep funding costs high.
singapore.thesis_status: Structurally bearish — NIM compression from dual-channel headwinds, tactically overbought.
singapore.key_development: Singapore's Q2 GDP grew 5.7% (per CNBC), beating expectations and lifting bank shares further, but the fundamental NIM relief trigger (US10Y sustainably below 4.20%) remains unmet.

semiconductors.summary: The cluster snapped a two-day losing streak as SOXX rallied 2.9%, led by memory names (MU +6.8%, 000660.KS +12%) and optics (TSEM +15%). NVDA's bounce to near its 50-day is the most actionable setup, but the overall cluster remains split between overbought high-beta names (AMD, LITE) and under-owned value plays (INTC, MU). The memory supercycle is the dominant theme; ASML's post-earnings jump could add another leg.
semiconductors.thesis_status: Structurally bullish — AI capex cycle intact, tactically stretched in select names.
semiconductors.key_development: KeyBanc's MU PT raise and TSEM's Japan photonics grant reinforced the memory/optics demand thesis, while the TSMC earnings preview tomorrow (Jul 16) is the next cluster-level event.

big_tech.summary: The cluster was flat with AMZN +0.3%, GOOG +1.8%, MSFT -1.2%, and PLTR +3.8%. The primary headwind remains the unresolved Microsoft-OpenAI credit circularity and a broader rotation out of mega-cap software into hardware. GOOG's July 22 earnings are the next catalyst — a TPU beat could lift the whole cloud-centric part of the cluster.
big_tech.thesis_status: Mixed — capex confirmed, but credit-circularity and regulatory risks remain.
big_tech.key_development: OpenAI breakup chatter resurfaced (per Bundle), reviving MSFT's Azure concentration concerns, while New York's AI data center ban (per Motley Fool) signals growing regulatory risk.

interconnected.name: Coherent
interconnected.reason: NVDA's $2B photonics investment twin — Coherent's InP capacity doubling and Sherman TX fab mirror LITE's buildout, directly tied to the AI optical interconnect thesis.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if COHR pulls back 8-10% from its current level toward its 200-day, aligning with the next LITE earnings catalyst on Aug 11.

interconnected.name: Marvell Technology
interconnected.reason: Custom ASIC and optical DSP leader, directly benefiting from the same hyperscaler custom-silicon ramp as AVGO and the optical interconnect surge as LITE/TSEM.
interconnected.entry_note: Revisit after Google's Jul 22 earnings for TPU order book confirmation — a beat could lift MRVL's datacenter segment.

interconnected.name: Arista Networks
interconnected.reason: Primary switch supplier for NVIDIA Spectrum-X back-end networks; any NVDA Vera Rubin volume ramp directly increases ANET's AI networking revenue.
interconnected.entry_note: Watch on a pullback to its 50-day ahead of the Jul 22 Google earnings, which could provide capex read-through for the broader networking stack.

active_risks: US reinstated its naval blockade on Iran and proposed a 20% transit fee on Hormuz shipping, while CENTCOM continued strikes. Two UAE tankers were hit by Iranian cruise missiles, and Hormuz traffic is near lowest levels in two months.
Yemen's Houthi forces could expand the conflict into the Bab al-Mandeb strait, threatening a second major energy chokepoint and further disrupting global shipping.
CPI inflation unexpectedly fell to 3.7% YoY in June (FRED), easing Fed rate-hike pressure, but oil's surge may offset the disinflationary impulse and keep the July FOMC meeting a live policy event.

US-China tech tensions escalated as China halted helium exports again and the NSF restricted research collaboration with Chinese universities, while Trump eased export controls for the UAE, signaling selective trade flexibility.
geopolitical.market_impact: Elevated oil prices are a direct cost headwind for AI infrastructure and memory makers like MU and INTC, while the dollar's slight weakness aids Asian semiconductor exporters. The NASDAQ's recovery in the face of Hormuz risk suggests the AI capex thesis is absorbing the energy shock for now, but any further escalation that pushes WTI above $85 would likely force a risk-off rotation out of high-multiple semis names.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: CPI release (June): 3.7% YoY, core 2.6%, both well below expectations. ASML's post-earnings +1.8% after-hours move signals that the semis supply chain is largely looking through the geopolitical noise.

base.description: Hormuz stalemate holds, WTI oscillates $78–$85; the AI capex cycle continues as hyperscaler earnings reassure, but the VIX stays elevated around 16–18. Singapore banks correct from overbought levels, and semis drift sideways ahead of Q2 prints.

optimistic.description: A US-Iran ceasefire is reached, WTI retreats to the low $70s, and the Fed holds rates in July. The semis cluster rallies 5–10% as the energy premium unwinds, with NVDA and MU leading a second leg higher.

pessimistic.description: Hormuz blockade tightens further, WTI breaks above $90, and the Fed is forced to hike. Semis sell off 8–12% as input costs and recession fears bite; the Singapore banks give back all July gains, falling 8–10%.

wildcard.description: Yemen's Houthis close the Bab al-Mandeb, causing a dual-chokepoint energy shock and a scramble for supply. WTI spikes above $100, global equities shed 5% in a week, and defensive positioning causes a flight from risk assets into gold and the dollar.

geopolitical.portfolio_action: Hold current positions; the CPI cooldown buys the Fed time, but the Hormuz tail risk is not priced out. Deploy new capital only into well-supported names with clear earnings catalysts, and avoid energy-sensitive plays until the Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizes.

portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: 7 of 31 names are hard-blocked on technical extension, and while the CPI print eased rate-hike fears, the Hormuz blockade keeps oil above $80—a direct cost risk for the semis-heavy portfolio. The near-term path hinges on hyperscaler earnings starting July 22; until then, favor watching over deploying.
